---
title: "Cluster Randomized Trials: Methods for Reach and Impact"
date-modified: last-modified
---

::: {.callout-note .draft}
**Draft scaffold.** This site is under active development. Year-1 content
covers Foundations (chapters 1–4) and two hands-on chapters that drive the
[`TwoStageTMLE`](https://github.com/LauraBalzer/TwoStageTMLE) R package
end-to-end. Effect heterogeneity, the Roadmap walker Shiny app, and the
SAPPHIRE / Peer-led DCP worked examples will land in Years 2–4.
:::

## Welcome

Cluster randomized trials (CRTs) randomize *groups* — communities, clinics,
schools, health systems — rather than individuals. They are the design of
choice when interventions act at the group level: training community health
workers, changing a clinic's care-delivery model, deploying a public-health
campaign. CRTs are increasingly used to evaluate **HIV prevention
strategies** that aim to both reach a target population *and* improve
outcomes among those reached.

Compared with individually-randomized trials, CRTs face three statistical
challenges that standard methods handle poorly:

1. **Few independent units.** Most CRTs randomize fewer than 40 clusters.
2. **Missing outcomes** are nearly universal, and the missingness mechanism
   is often on the causal pathway (e.g., screening that itself depends on
   the intervention).
3. **Effect heterogeneity** at the cluster-, sub-cluster-, and
   individual-level is rarely evaluated well — established subgroups miss
   data-driven insights, and naïve stratification is severely
   underpowered.

This site presents **Two-Stage TMLE** [@balzer2021twostage] —
a targeted machine-learning estimator that addresses all three challenges
in a single framework. The estimator separates the per-cluster
missing-data adjustment (Stage 1) from the between-cluster effect
estimation (Stage 2), and is **doubly robust**, **efficient**, and
**preserves Type-I error control** with few independent units.

## What you will learn

By the end of this site, participants will be able to:

1. State a CRT research question using the **Causal Roadmap**
   [@petersen2014causalroadmap] and identify the multilevel data
   structure and missing-data mechanism.
2. **Run Two-Stage TMLE** on a CRT with missing outcomes using the
   `TwoStageTMLE` R package — including Adaptive Pre-specification.
3. **Diagnose** when the *multilevel-mediation-missing data problem*
   applies (post-baseline screening mediates the effect) and use the
   Nakato (2026) ratio-based Stage 1 estimator instead.
4. **Write a Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP)** for a CRT analysis that
   pre-specifies the Super Learner library, the candidate adjustment
   set, and the inference scheme.

## How to use this site

The chapters are organised in two arcs:

* **Foundations** (chapters 1–3) — conceptual material: why CRTs need
  special methods, the Causal Roadmap, and the three core challenges.
* **Two-Stage TMLE** (chapters 4–6) — the method itself, plus two
  hands-on chapters that run real code against simulated CRTs.

Each R-coded chapter is reproducible: code chunks are executable and the
data live alongside the chapter. Reading time per chapter: ~20 minutes.

## Acknowledgements

This work is supported by the National Institutes of Health (PI: Laura
Balzer, UC Berkeley). The site is built with [Quarto](https://quarto.org/)
and draws on the design of three earlier sites — the
[`lmtp` workshop](https://www.beyondtheate.com/), the
[NIH Collaboratory Rethinking Clinical Trials Living
Textbook](https://rethinkingclinicaltrials.org/), and the
[CTML Introduction to Causal
Inference](https://ctml.berkeley.edu/introduction-causal-inference).
